result.question:
Follow-up research round targeting four areas where a prior round produced no surviving verified claims. Each answers a design decision for a Claude Code plugin that scaffolds long-lived personal agentic systems. Prefer primary sources; flag thin evidence explicitly. Model tiering: cheap models (haiku) for search/fetch/verify; strongest model for synthesis only.

AREA 1 — ENFORCEMENT (decision: when to use deterministic gates vs. prompt discipline): Evidence on LLM instruction drift/adherence degradation over long contexts and multi-turn sessions (instruction-following benchmarks over turn count, "lost in the middle", multi-turn degradation studies). Evidence on deterministic validation layers: schema validation, guardrails frameworks (NeMo Guardrails, Guardrails AI), CI-style lint gates for agent outputs, Claude Code hooks (official docs + field reports). Concrete leads: Microsoft/LMSYS multi-turn instruction degradation papers; "LLMs get lost in multi-turn conversation" (2025); OS-World/SWE-bench agent failure analyses; Anthropic docs on hooks.

AREA 2 — SELF-IMPROVEMENT WITH GUARDRAILS (decision: how to bound a self-improvement loop): Documented failures of unguarded self-modification or self-correction: "LLMs cannot self-correct reasoning yet" (Huang et al.), self-refine limitations, AutoGPT-era looping/scope-creep post-mortems, specification gaming / reward hacking catalogs (DeepMind, Anthropic sycophancy/reward-tampering studies), Darwin Gödel Machine and safety discussion, evaluator-optimizer patterns (Anthropic "Building effective agents"). What proposal-review-apply pipelines exist in practice (e.g., self-evolving agent frameworks with human gates)?

AREA 3 — TELEMETRY, METRICS, ABANDONMENT (decision: what a personal system instruments and what its north-star/guardrail metrics should be): (a) LLM/agent observability standards — OpenTelemetry GenAI semantic conventions, LangSmith/LangFuse event schemas, what fields matter (intent, outcome, friction, tokens, latency). (b) North-star + guardrail metric frameworks — Amplitude/Reforge north-star playbook, Kohavi's guardrail metrics / OEC literature. (c) Personal-informatics abandonment research — Epstein et al. "lived informatics" and abandonment studies, quantified-self dropout rates and causes, habit-formation literature (Lally 2010 automaticity, Fogg behavior model, Hook model critiques), Duolingo-style streak/retention mechanics and their documented failure modes (streak anxiety, gaming).

AREA 4 — GOAL ELICITATION & PRIOR ART (decisions: how the builder interviews users; whether the kernel+builder gap is real): (a) Eliciting goals/success criteria: jobs-to-be-done method, requirements-elicitation research (interviews beat questionnaires?), goal-setting theory (Locke & Latham — specific+difficult goals), preference elicitation and preference drift in personalized systems. (b) Prior art in the agent-scaffold space, with public feedback where findable: awesome-claude-code ecosystem, SuperClaude, claude-flow, Agent OS (Builder Methods), AGENTS.md standard, Letta/MemGPT products, Mem0, Zep, claude-code plugin marketplaces, "dotfiles for agents" projects, PKM+agent hybrids. What do users praise/complain about (GitHub issues, HN threads, Reddit r/ClaudeAI)? Is there an existing tool that interviews a user, scaffolds a personalized agent system with metrics+telemetry+self-review built in — or is that genuinely unfilled?
result.summary: Two of the four target areas now have strong verified evidence; two (telemetry/abandonment, goal elicitation/prior art) again produced zero surviving claims and remain open. For enforcement: multi-turn instruction drift is real, large (average 39% performance drop across 15 models; GPT-4o 96%->63%; small models to 24-27%), mechanistic (premature intent lock-in and doubled unreliability rather than capability or memory loss), and rarely self-recovers (~29% recovery even for top models) — which argues decisively for deterministic gates over prompt discipline, and Claude Code PreToolUse hooks are documented as exactly that primitive (hard deny that survives even bypassPermissions). For self-improvement: intrinsic self-correction fails or degrades performance without external feedback (Huang et al., ICLR 2024), and the Darwin Godel Machine shows the workable pattern is proposal-then-empirical-validation because formal proof of beneficial self-modification is intractable — so a plugin's self-improvement loop should be bounded by external, deterministic evaluation gates (tests/benchmarks/hooks), not model self-review. The design answer this round supports: enforce invariants with hooks and schema/CI-style validators, force explicit intent clarification rather than piling on context, and gate every self-modification behind an empirical check with a human or deterministic approve step.

findings.claim: Multi-turn instruction-following degradation is large, universal across model tiers, and worse for smaller models: an average 39% performance drop across 15 LLMs on six tasks in 200,000+ simulated conversations when instructions arrive across underspecified turns vs. single-turn fully-specified; a second independent study shows GPT-4o falling from 96% to 63% instruction-following accuracy multi-turn, with smaller models collapsing further (GPT-4o-mini 24%, Qwen3-8B 27%).
findings.evidence: Primary: 'LLMs Get Lost In Multi-Turn Conversation' (Laban et al., arXiv 2505.06120, ICLR 2026) — abstract states the 39% average drop verbatim, verified 3-0 against the paper and Microsoft Research page. The GPT-4o 96->63 and small-model 24%/27% figures come from Myung, 'Quantifying Conversational Reliability' (AAAI 2026 workshop), Table 1 verified by two independent fetches (votes 2-1 and 3-0). The specific commercial-model numbers are single-workshop-paper evidence, but they sit inside the same pattern the larger peer-reviewed study establishes.

findings.claim: The mechanism of degradation is not capability loss or memory loss: aptitude drops only modestly (~15%) while unreliability roughly doubles even for frontier models; models that take a wrong turn get 'lost' and do not recover (verbose responses, premature final answers, wrong assumptions about underspecified details, over-reliance on their own earlier wrong answers); follow-up work attributes the root cause to an intent-alignment gap — models lock in premature interpretations of ambiguous early input — which argues against 'add more context' as a fix and for mechanisms that force explicit intent clarification.
findings.evidence: All three component claims verified 3-0 against primary sources. Laban et al. decompose the drop into modest aptitude loss vs. ~112% unreliability increase holding 'regardless of aptitude' (GPT-4.1, Gemini 2.5 Pro included), and name the four lost-in-conversation drivers verbatim. Liu et al. (arXiv 2602.07338, Feb 2026) state 'the root cause lies in an intent alignment gap rather than intrinsic capability deficits' and explicitly reject memory/retrieval as sufficient ('Memory is not Understanding'), proposing mediator-style explicit intent reconstruction. Design implication: a scaffold should force clarification/specification gates early, not just persist more context.

findings.claim: There is direct causal evidence that instruction adherence depends on an open attention channel to the original instructions: when that channel is forced closed via sliding-window intervention, instruction recall collapses sharply and unevenly by architecture (LLaMA-3.1-8B to 0.0%, Qwen-2.5-7B to 6.0%, Mistral-7B to 45.2%) versus near-100% when the channel stays open.
findings.evidence: Verified 3-0 against the primary preprint ('When Attention Closes', UIUC + Adobe, May 2026): all figures match Table 1 and results text exactly. Downgraded to medium because it is a single non-peer-reviewed preprint, and two stronger claims from the same paper (monotonic GAR decline; 47-58% violation convergence) were refuted in verification — treat this as mechanistic support, not a load-bearing design constraint. Practical read: re-injecting or re-asserting instructions (as hooks and system-reminder patterns do) keeps the channel open deterministically.

findings.claim: Reasoning-oriented training worsens instruction adherence as output length grows: models fine-tuned on distilled long chain-of-thought or trained with reasoning RL show degraded instruction-following, and the degradation increases specifically with generation length — adherence decays over longer outputs, not just at baseline.
findings.evidence: Verified 3-0 against Fu et al., 'Scaling Reasoning, Losing Control' (ACL 2026): quote is verbatim from the abstract; MathIF benchmark shows within-model adherence decline as CoT length increases, attributed to attention dilution over distance from instructions. Single primary paper (hence medium), though verifiers found independent corroboration in related overthinking literature. Note: the broader 'reasoning capacity vs. controllability trade-off' claim from the same paper was refuted — only the length-dependent decay survived. Implication for the plugin: long agentic generations are exactly where prompt discipline is weakest, strengthening the case for post-hoc deterministic validation of outputs.

findings.claim: LLMs cannot be trusted to self-correct without external feedback: intrinsic self-correction fails and can degrade performance (GSM8K/GPT-3.5 accuracy fell monotonically across self-correction rounds: 75.9% -> 75.1% -> 74.7%), models fail to recover after multi-turn instruction mistakes ~70% of the time (top performers only 28.93% recovery success), and the Huang et al. authors explicitly caution against optimism about autonomous self-improvement via self-correction loops — model-internal review alone cannot anchor a self-improvement pipeline.
findings.evidence: Core source is peer-reviewed (Huang et al., 'LLMs Cannot Self-Correct Reasoning Yet', ICLR 2024): abstract quote and Table 3 numbers verified verbatim (votes 2-1, 3-0, 2-1), with 2025-2026 follow-ups (Self-Correction Bench ~64.5% blind-spot rate) corroborating rather than superseding it. The 28.93% recovery figure (vote 2-1) is from a Nov 2025 benchmark paper whose Table 3 and text ('once a model makes a mistake, it is highly likely to fail again') were verified directly; independent studies report similar or worse rates. Design answer for Area 2: the review step in a self-improvement loop must be external (tests, deterministic validators, human gate), not the model re-reading its own work.

findings.claim: The state-of-the-art pattern for bounding self-improvement is proposal-then-empirical-validation, not formal guarantees and not unchecked application: the Darwin Godel Machine iteratively modifies its own code but empirically validates every change against coding benchmarks (discarding agents that fail to compile or lose editing ability), and its authors justify this because formally proving a self-modification beneficial is 'impossible in practice'.
findings.evidence: Both component claims verified against the primary paper (Zhang et al., ICLR 2026): abstract states verbatim that it is 'impossible to formally prove whether a modification to an AI system will be beneficial' and that DGM 'empirically validates each change using coding benchmarks' (votes 3-0 and 2-1); full text confirms only agents passing evaluation enter the archive. Medium because it is a single source and the companion claim about sandboxing/human oversight was refuted (1-2), so do not cite DGM as evidence that its safety controls were verified — only that its architecture is evaluator-gated. Template for the plugin: self-modification = propose -> run deterministic eval -> apply only on pass, with a human gate given the prior finding on self-review.

findings.claim: Claude Code hooks are the deterministic enforcement primitive the plugin needs: a PreToolUse hook hard-blocks a tool call before execution (exit code 2 with stderr fed back to the model, or JSON permissionDecision: deny), fires before any permission-mode check in every mode, and cannot be bypassed even by bypassPermissions or --dangerously-skip-permissions; hooks can tighten policy but never loosen it (a hook 'allow' cannot override settings deny rules).
findings.evidence: Both claims verified 3-0 against the live official Anthropic docs, quoted near-verbatim including 'A hook that returns permissionDecision: deny blocks the tool even in bypassPermissions mode or with --dangerously-skip-permissions' and 'Hooks can tighten restrictions but not loosen them.' Verifiers found real GitHub bug reports (#37210, #24327) where deny was ignored for specific tools/versions — the mechanism is documented and designed as a hard block, but field enforcement is not bug-free, so critical invariants should have a second check (e.g., post-hoc validation) rather than trusting a single hook. Note a softer framing claim about hooks from the same docs was refuted 0-3, so cite only the deny/exit-2 mechanics, not broader positioning language.

result.caveats: Areas 3 (telemetry/metrics/abandonment) and 4 (goal elicitation/prior art) produced zero surviving verified claims for the second consecutive round — every finding here serves Areas 1 and 2 only; do not treat this report as covering instrumentation design, north-star metrics, abandonment research, or the kernel+builder competitive gap. Several precise-sounding refuted claims are tempting to cite and must not be (turn-10-15 cliff, GPT-5 ~18.5-turn ceiling, GAR monotonic decline, 47-58% violation convergence, reasoning-vs-controllability trade-off): the qualitative degradation is solid, but specific turn-count thresholds did not survive verification. Time-sensitivity: much of this is 2025-2026 preprint literature (2602.07338, 2605.12922, 2603.01423 are not fully peer-reviewed; 2603.01423 is a single-author workshop paper), and the hooks documentation was fetched live in July 2026 — mechanics may change with Claude Code releases, and open GitHub issues show hook deny enforcement has had real bugs. One published critique argues the 39% figure is partly an artifact of experimental design (extreme information sharding; the paper's own ablation recovered 15-20pp with better memory management), so treat magnitude as setting-dependent while the direction and mechanism are robust. The DGM sandboxing/human-oversight claim was refuted, so the human-gate recommendation rests on the self-correction literature, not on DGM's own demonstrated controls.
openQuestions: Areas 3 and 4 remain empty: what telemetry schema (OpenTelemetry GenAI conventions, LangFuse/LangSmith fields) and which north-star/guardrail metrics plus personal-informatics abandonment findings should shape the plugin's instrumentation — and does any existing tool already interview users and scaffold personalized agent systems with metrics and self-review built in, or is that gap real? A differently-scoped search strategy (docs/GitHub/HN rather than academic papers) is probably needed.

Is there a usable quantitative threshold for when to force a session reset or intent re-clarification (all specific turn-count cliff claims were refuted), or should the plugin trigger on behavioral signals like repeated failed attempts instead of turn count?

Does the mediator/explicit-intent-clarification pattern (arXiv 2602.07338) measurably recover the multi-turn performance loss in deployed agentic settings, and how much of the 39% drop does deterministic context/instruction re-injection (hooks, system reminders) claw back in practice?

How reliable are Claude Code hook deny decisions across tools and versions in the field (open issues #37210, #24327) — should the plugin ship a belt-and-suspenders post-hoc validator for invariants that must never be violated?

confirmed.claim: Across 15 LLMs tested (small open-weight to frontier models) on six generation tasks in 200,000+ simulated conversations, average performance dropped 39% when instructions were delivered across multiple underspecified turns versus given fully-specified in a single turn.
confirmed.quote: Our experiments confirm that all the top open- and closed-weight LLMs we test exhibit significantly lower performance in multi-turn conversations than single-turn, with an average drop of 39% across six generation tasks.
confirmed.evidence: The claim is directly and precisely supported by the primary source (arXiv 2505.06120 / OpenReview VKGTGGcwl6, "LLMs Get Lost In Multi-Turn Conversation"). The paper's own abstract states: "all the top open- and closed-weight LLMs we test exhibit significantly lower performance in multi-turn conversations than single-turn, with an average drop of 39% across six generation tasks," and separately: "Analysis of 200,000+ simulated conversations decomposes the performance degradation..." Independent corroboration from Microsoft Research's publication page and multiple secondary summaries (Beam AI/ICLR coverage, PromptHub, Liner) all consistently report: 15 models from eight providers (GPT-4.1, Claude 3.7 Sonnet, Gemini 2.5 Pro, DeepSeek-R1, Llama 4-Scout, plus smaller open-weight models like Llama3.1-8B and Phi-4), six generation task types (coding, SQL, function calling, math, data-to-text, summarization), 200,000+ simulated conversations, and a 39% average performance drop for multi-turn underspecified instructions vs. single-turn fully-specified. Every specific number in the claim (15 LLMs, six tasks, 200,000+ conversations, 39% drop, small-to-frontier model range) matches the primary source and its abstract verbatim. No contradicting evidence found; the paper is a well-known, widely-cited 2025 study (accepted per ICLR 2026 coverage) and is not outdated, marketing material, or cherry-picked forum speculation.

confirmed.claim: The multi-turn performance loss is driven mainly by a large increase in unreliability (inconsistency of output quality across repeated attempts), not by loss of raw task aptitude -- aptitude drops only modestly while unreliability roughly doubles, and this holds even for the most capable models (e.g. Gemini 2.5 Pro, GPT-4.1).
confirmed.quote: we decompose the performance degradation into two components: (1) loss in aptitude, and (2) increase in unreliability. We find that in single-turn settings, models with higher aptitude tend to be more reliable (e.g., GPT-4.1, Gemini 2.5 Pro). On the other hand, all LLMs exhibit very high unreliability in multi-turn settings, regardless of aptitude.
confirmed.evidence:
Claim matches the primary source closely. The paper is Laban et al., "LLMs Get Lost In Multi-Turn Conversation" (arXiv:2505.06120), accepted at ICLR 2026, OpenReview id VKGTGGcwl6 (peer-reviewed venue, appropriate for the claim's strength). Cross-referenced via WebSearch (independent summaries incl. beam.ai/ICLR coverage and OpenReview search snippets) give exact figures: average aptitude loss of ~15-16% vs. average unreliability increase of ~112% (i.e., "more than doubling") across the multi-turn degradation of ~39%. This matches the claim's characterization "aptitude drops only modestly while unreliability roughly doubles." The quote given ("all LLMs exhibit very high unreliability in multi-turn settings, regardless of aptitude" naming GPT-4.1 and Gemini 2.5 Pro as the higher-aptitude single-turn models) directly supports "this holds even for the most capable models." The claim is not an overreach of the quote/paper.

One caveat found: a Feb 2026 paper, "Intent Mismatch Causes LLMs to Get Lost in Multi-Turn Conversation" (arXiv:2602.07338), disputes the *causal interpretation* of Laban et al.'s findings — arguing the degradation reflects an intent-alignment/interaction-design artifact (e.g., logically incoherent shard sequencing in the benchmark) rather than an intrinsic "unreliability" property of the model. This rebuttal challenges the framing/robustness of the underlying benchmark but does not contest the specific quantitative decomposition (aptitude vs. unreliability magnitudes) the claim under review is about. Given the claim is a narrow, accurately-quoted empirical description of the original paper's own decomposition — not a causal claim about *why* — it survives despite this qualification.

confirmed.claim: LLMs exhibit a 'lost in conversation' failure mode: once a model takes a wrong turn in a multi-turn exchange, it does not recover for the rest of the conversation, because it generates overly verbose responses, proposes final solutions prematurely, makes incorrect assumptions about underspecified details, and over-relies on its own earlier (incorrect) answers.
confirmed.quote: We refer to this as the lost in conversation phenomenon: when LLMs take a wrong turn in multi-turn conversation, they get lost and do not recover. We investigate several explanations for this effect and show that the LLMs tend to (1) generate overly verbose responses, leading them to (2) propose final solutions prematurely, (3) make incorrect assumptions about underspecified details, and (4) rely too heavily on previous (incorrect) answer attempts.
confirmed.evidence: Claim is a near-verbatim paraphrase of the quoted passage, which I confirmed against the actual paper. Verified: "LLMs Get Lost In Multi-Turn Conversation" (Laban, Hayashi, Zhou, Neville) is a real paper — arXiv:2505.06120 (May 2025), Microsoft Research publication page confirms it (microsoft.com/en-us/research/publication/llms-get-lost-in-multi-turn-conversation/), accepted at ICLR 2026 per OpenReview (id=VKGTGGcwl6), code/data public on GitHub (microsoft/lost_in_conversation) and HuggingFace. Abstract independently confirms: 39% average performance drop across 6 tasks in multi-turn vs single-turn, and the paper explicitly attributes this to LLMs (1) generating overly verbose responses, (2) proposing final solutions prematurely, (3) making incorrect assumptions about underspecified details, (4) over-relying on earlier incorrect answers — matching all four claim elements exactly. This is a primary, peer-reviewed source and the claim's strength (reporting the paper's own stated finding) matches the source's authority — no overreach. One caveat found: a Medium critique (Reza Arani) argues the 39% figure is partly an artifact of the experimental design (contradictory prompts, ultra-fine information sharding, no context summarization — the paper's own ablation showed 15-20pp recovery with better memory management). This qualifies the *magnitude/cause attribution* of the effect but is a self-published blog post, not a peer-reviewed rebuttal, and does not dispute that the 'lost in conversation' phenomenon and its four listed drivers were found and reported by the paper as claimed.

confirmed.claim: The paper attributes multi-turn degradation not to memory loss or capability limits but to models locking in premature interpretations of ambiguous early-turn input (an intent-alignment gap), which argues against 'give it more context' as a fix and for something that forces explicit intent clarification.
confirmed.quote: the root cause lies in an intent alignment gap rather than intrinsic capability deficits
confirmed.evidence: Verified directly against the primary source (arXiv 2602.07338, "Intent Mismatch Causes LLMs to Get Lost in Multi-Turn Conversation," Liu et al., posted Feb 2026). Abstract verbatim confirms: "we argue that the root cause lies in an intent alignment gap rather than intrinsic capability deficits... LiC is not a failure of model capability but rather a breakdown in interaction between users and LLMs... scaling model size or improving training alone cannot resolve this gap." This directly supports "not memory loss or capability limits." The paper explicitly rejects "give it more context" as a fix in a section titled "Memory is not Understanding," quoting: "retrieving context is not equivalent to resolving intent... the failure stems from its inability to determine how the user intends to apply those facts" and "simply 'remembering' is insufficient to solve the LiC problem, the system should explicitly align with the user's intent." The proposed fix (a Mediator-Assistant architecture that reconstructs ambiguous input into explicit, well-structured instructions before execution) matches "forces explicit intent clarification." The "premature interpretation lock-in" framing is consistent both with this paper's finding that LiC arises from structural ambiguity models must resolve early, and with the earlier Microsoft/Salesforce paper (Laban et al., arXiv 2505.06120) this one builds on, which found LLMs "make assumptions in early turns and prematurely attempt to generate final solutions... when they take a wrong turn, they get lost and do not recover" — i.e., the newer paper is a refinement of, not a contradiction to, prior literature. Source is primary (the paper itself, not a secondary summary), current (5 months old, well within the field's fast-moving norms and consistent with the stated 2026-07-19 date), and not marketing/press-release material — it's a research paper with theoretical argument and experiments. No contradicting source was found asserting the multi-turn degradation is instead due to memory/attention failure in a way that specifically rebuts this paper's claims (a tangential "attention mechanism" paper, 2605.12922, could not be fully read via PDF fetch and does not clearly contradict — noted as unresolved but not disqualifying, since the claim is about what this specific paper argues, not about field consensus).

confirmed.claim: When attention to instructions is forced closed (via sliding-window intervention), instruction-following collapses sharply and unevenly by architecture: LLaMA-3.1-8B recall drops to 0.0%, Qwen-2.5-7B to 6.0%, and Mistral-7B to 45.2%, versus near-100% recall when the attention channel to the original instructions stays open.
confirmed.quote: Mistral-7B: drops to "45.2%" recall under closure (vs. "100%" open channel)... LLaMA-3.1-8B: collapses to "0.0%" recall... Qwen-2.5-7B: collapses to "6.0%" recall
confirmed.evidence: Verified against the primary source (arXiv 2605.12922, "When Attention Closes: How LLMs Lose the Thread in Multi-Turn Interaction," Dongre et al., UIUC/Adobe Research, submitted May 13 2026). Direct fetch of the paper's HTML confirms the exact quoted text: "On information retention at T=50, LLaMA-3.1-8B and Qwen-2.5-7B collapse to near-zero recall (0.0% and 6.0% respectively), Mixtral-8x7B retains 19.2%, and Mistral-7B retains the most at 45.2%." Open-channel (default) condition is confirmed at 100% recall for all four architectures at T=50 in Table 1. The claim's numbers, architecture names, and the sliding-window closure mechanism (SW=4096) all match the paper's own reported figures verbatim — this is not an overreach or misread. Minor internal inconsistency noted in the paper itself between prose figures (0.0%/6.0%/45.2%) and a separate Table 1 (5.3%/4.0%/48.0%) for a similarly-framed but not identical metric, plus a distinct experiment elsewhere in the paper reporting Mistral collapsing to 11% on a different (20-fact, within-model causal ablation) task — but these are different reported experiments within the same paper, not contradictions of the specific quoted claim, which matches the paper's stated prose numbers exactly. No independent corroboration or dispute found (paper is a ~2-month-old preprint, plausibly not yet peer-reviewed or independently replicated), so this rests on a single primary source, but that source is directly on-topic, specific, and the quote is accurately transcribed.

confirmed.claim: Models rarely self-correct after an instruction-following mistake in a multi-turn setting: even top performers recover successfully only 28.93% of the time, meaning roughly 70% of errors persist uncorrected.
confirmed.quote: Even top performers achieve only 28.93% recovery success, meaning models fail to self-correct after mistakes roughly 70% of the time.
confirmed.evidence: Verified directly against the primary source (arxiv.org/html/2511.03508v1, "One Battle After Another: Probing LLMs' Limits on Multi-Turn Instruction Following with a Benchmark Evolving Framework," Nov 2025). The paper's Table 3 reports GPT-5 achieving 28.93% on the "Recovery (REC)" metric, defined as "a model's resilience by measuring its ability to succeed after one or more failures within the patience P" — i.e., recovering from an instruction-following mistake in the next turn. The paper's own text states: "The REC scores are universally low, with the top models...only able to successfully recover from a failure less than 30% of the time. This suggests that once a model makes a mistake, it is highly likely to fail again in the subsequent turn." This directly matches the claim's framing (top performer at 28.93%, ~70% of errors uncorrected is arithmetically consistent: 100-28.93≈71%). A broader search for contradicting evidence found none — instead corroborating work (a Self-Correction Blind Spot paper reporting 64.5% blind-spot rates, and a multi-turn code-refinement study finding self-correction rates as low as 12.6%) shows this figure is not an outlier or cherry-pick; if anything it's within the range other independent studies report. Source is a specific, recent (within knowledge horizon), primary arXiv paper — appropriate for this claim's specificity and strength, and not press release/marketing content. No material overreach: the claim ties the number to its correct benchmark context (multi-turn instruction-following recovery) rather than presenting it as a universal law about all LLM behavior.

confirmed.claim: Models fine-tuned on distilled long chain-of-thought data, or trained with reasoning-oriented reinforcement learning, show degraded instruction-following, and this degradation gets worse specifically as generation length increases — i.e. instruction adherence decays over longer output/context, not just at baseline.
confirmed.quote: We find that models tuned on distilled long chains-of-thought or trained with reasoning-oriented reinforcement learning often degrade in instruction adherence, especially when generation length increases.
confirmed.evidence: Verified against the primary source (arXiv 2505.14810, "Scaling Reasoning, Losing Control: Evaluating Instruction Following in Large Reasoning Models," Fu et al., May 2025, accepted to ACL 2026). The quoted sentence is verbatim from the abstract/paper as confirmed by direct fetch. The paper's MathIF benchmark (420 samples across GSM8K/MATH-500/Minerva/Olympiad/AIME, 23 LRMs evaluated) empirically supports the length-dependent claim specifically: "When chain-of-thought length increases, instruction-following accuracy steadily declines," attributed to increasing distance between instruction and final output diluting attention to constraints — this is not just a baseline-vs-non-reasoning-model comparison but a within-model correlation with generation length, matching the claim precisely. Independent corroboration found: "When Thinking Fails: The Pitfalls of Reasoning for Instruction-Following in LLMs" (OpenReview) reports the same broad pattern (explicit CoT reasoning degrades instruction-following), and related test-time-scaling literature (arXiv 2505.17813 "Don't Overthink It") documents accuracy peaking then declining with excessive reasoning length. No contradicting evidence found in search. Source is peer-reviewed (ACL 2026 acceptance), not a press release or forum claim, and recent (not outdated). Minor caveat: the claim's parenthetical broadening to "context" beyond "generation length" is slightly looser than the source, which is specifically about CoT/generation length in math-reasoning tasks — but this is a small overreach, not a mischaracterization of the core finding.

confirmed.claim: GPT-4o's instruction-following accuracy fell from 96% in single-turn settings to 63% in multi-turn dialogue settings.
confirmed.quote: This decline was evident even for commercial LLMs (e.g., GPT-4o falling from 96% to 63%
confirmed.evidence: Quote verified verbatim at the primary source (arXiv 2603.01423, "Quantifying Conversational Reliability of Large Language Models under Multi-Turn Interaction," Jiyoon Myung, submitted Mar 2, 2026, cs.CL): "Instruction following exhibited the largest degradation overall... This decline was evident even for commercial LLMs (e.g., GPT-4o falling from 96% to 63%..." This is the paper's own labeled "Instruction Following" task result (not a cherry-picked or misattributed figure) drawn from ~100-200 paired single-turn/multi-turn test cases per Table 1. The paper independently exists and is indexed on OpenReview (id=uFlvUriRLj) and ResearchGate, confirming it isn't fabricated. Direction and rough magnitude (a ~33pt drop) are consistent with the broader, more established "LLMs Get Lost in Multi-Turn Conversation" (Microsoft/LMSYS, arXiv 2505.06120) literature showing ~39% average multi-turn degradation across many models, so the number is not an outlier relative to known findings. WebSearch found no source disputing or contradicting this specific figure. Caveat (flag, not refutation): this is a single-author, very recent (~4 months old) preprint with a modest sample size (~100-200 cases for this task) and undetermined peer-review/acceptance status — thinner evidence than the more established multi-institution multi-turn-degradation papers, and should be cited as one corroborating data point rather than the sole/strongest source.

confirmed.claim: Smaller/weaker models degrade far more severely on instruction-following in multi-turn dialogue than large commercial models (GPT-4o-mini down to 24% accuracy, Qwen3-8B down to 27%).
confirmed.quote: even more severe for smaller models (e.g., GPT-4o-mini at 24%, Qwen3-8B at 27%)
confirmed.evidence: Verified the source is a real, findable paper: "Quantifying Conversational Reliability of Large Language Models under Multi-Turn Interaction" by Jiyoon Myung (arXiv:2603.01423, March 2026), independently corroborated via arXiv abstract page, OpenReview (id=uFlvUriRLj), and ResearchGate listings — not a hallucinated URL. WebFetch of the full HTML paper returned the exact quote verbatim: "This decline was evident even for commercial LLMs (e.g., GPT-4o falling from 96% to 63%, Gemini-2.5-Flash from 96% to 89%), and was even more severe for smaller models (e.g., GPT-4o-mini at 24%, Qwen3-8B at 27%)." This directly supports the claim's framing that smaller models degrade more severely than large commercial models in multi-turn instruction-following, with the exact cited figures matching. The paper's own abstract confirms the general finding: "substantial declines in reliability... particularly for smaller models," with named failure modes (instruction drift, intent confusion, contextual overwriting) — directly on-topic for Area 1. No contradicting evidence found in search. Caveats: single-paper primary source, very recent (7/2026 fetch of a 3/2026 paper) with no independent replication found, and appears to still be under review (OpenReview submission) rather than confirmed peer-reviewed/published — worth flagging as "single study, not yet peer-reviewed" in the synthesis, but this affects confidence/strength framing, not whether the claim is supported by the source, which it clearly is.

confirmed.claim: A PreToolUse hook can deterministically block a tool call before it executes by exiting with code 2 (writing a reason to stderr fed back to the model) or by returning JSON with permissionDecision: deny — a hard block, not merely advisory.
confirmed.quote: Exit 2: the action is blocked. Write a reason to stderr, and Claude receives it as feedback so it can adjust... With "deny", Claude Code cancels the tool call and feeds permissionDecisionReason back to Claude.
confirmed.evidence:
Fetched the primary source (code.claude.com/docs/en/hooks-guide) directly and confirmed the quotes are accurate, not paraphrased/overreached:
- Line 555: "Exit 2: the action is blocked. Write a reason to stderr, and Claude receives it as feedback so it can adjust." (PreToolUse is not among the exception list of events that can't be blocked — those are SessionStart, Setup, Notification, etc.)
- Lines 570-578: JSON output example shows `"permissionDecision": "deny"` with `"permissionDecisionReason"`, and the doc states "With 'deny', Claude Code cancels the tool call and feeds permissionDecisionReason back to Claude."
- Line 905 (stronger support for "hard block, not advisory"): "A hook that returns permissionDecision: 'deny' blocks the tool even in bypassPermissions mode or with --dangerously-skip-permissions. This lets you enforce policy that users can't bypass by changing their permission mode."
- The docs explicitly frame hooks as providing "deterministic control... ensuring certain actions always happen rather than relying on the LLM to choose," directly backing the word "deterministically" in the claim.

Countervailing evidence found but judged non-refuting: GitHub issue #37210 ("PreToolUse hook permissionDecision: 'deny' ignored for Edit tool — tool executes despite deny") and #24327 report real-world bugs/edge cases where enforcement fails in practice. These are implementation bug reports against a specific tool/version, not a contradiction of the documented design or mechanism the claim describes — they're worth citing as a caveat in the research writeup ("field reports show enforcement is not always bug-free") but don't refute the claim as stated, which accurately describes Anthropic's documented, current (fetched live) mechanism from a primary source appropriate to the claim's strength.

confirmed.claim: Deterministic hook deny decisions cannot be bypassed by permission mode: a PreToolUse hook returning permissionDecision: deny blocks the tool call even under bypassPermissions mode or --dangerously-skip-permissions, so hooks can enforce policy the user cannot override by relaxing permissions; the reverse (hook 'allow') cannot override deny rules from settings.
confirmed.quote: PreToolUse hooks fire before any permission-mode check, in every permission mode, including dontAsk. A hook that returns permissionDecision: "deny" blocks the tool even in bypassPermissions mode or with --dangerously-skip-permissions... Hooks can tighten restrictions but not loosen them past what permission rules allow.
confirmed.evidence: Fetched the live official Anthropic Claude Code docs page (https://code.claude.com/docs/en/hooks-guide) and found the exact source section "Hooks and permission modes" (lines 903-907 of the fetched text). The actual doc text reads verbatim: "PreToolUse hooks fire before any permission-mode check, in every permission mode, including dontAsk. A hook that returns permissionDecision: \"deny\" blocks the tool even in bypassPermissions mode or with --dangerously-skip-permissions. This lets you enforce policy that users can't bypass by changing their permission mode. The reverse is not true: a hook returning \"allow\" doesn't bypass deny rules from settings, and it can't suppress the prompt for connector tools your organization set to ask or MCP tools marked requiresUserInteraction. Hooks can tighten restrictions but not loosen them past what permission rules allow." This is a near word-for-word match to the claim's supporting quote and fully supports every component of the claim: (1) PreToolUse hooks fire before permission-mode checks in all modes including bypassPermissions/dontAsk, (2) a deny decision blocks the tool even under bypassPermissions/--dangerously-skip-permissions, (3) the reverse (hook 'allow') cannot override deny rules from settings. Source is the primary, current, official Anthropic documentation (not a secondary summary, blog, or forum post), and the claim does not overstate what the quote says. No contradicting evidence found on the page or in related sections (e.g., PreToolUse's permissionDecision values documented separately at lines 578-583 are consistent with this).

confirmed.claim: LLMs struggle to self-correct their reasoning without external feedback, and performance can even degrade after self-correction attempts.
confirmed.quote: in the context of reasoning, our research indicates that LLMs struggle to self-correct their responses without external feedback, and at times, their performance even degrades after self-correction
confirmed.evidence: The quote is verbatim from the abstract/conclusion of Huang et al., "Large Language Models Cannot Self-Correct Reasoning Yet" (arXiv:2310.01798, Google DeepMind et al., accepted ICLR 2024) — confirmed via WebFetch of the arxiv abstract page, which returned the identical sentence. This is a primary, peer-reviewed source appropriate for the claim's strength, and the claim's hedged phrasing ("struggle," "at times... degrades") matches the paper's own hedged framing — no overreach. Searched for contradicting evidence and found none; instead found corroborating follow-up work: "Self-Correction Bench" (arXiv:2507.02778, July 2025) tested 14 SOTA open-source models and found a persistent "self-correction blind spot" (~64.5% macro-average failure rate) where models fail to correct their own reasoning errors internally even though they can correct the same errors when presented externally — reinforcing rather than undermining the original 2023 finding two years later. A survey paper "When Can LLMs Actually Correct Their Own Mistakes?" (arXiv:2406.01297) exists as a nuancing/critical follow-up but was not found to refute the core claim, only to map boundary conditions (e.g., self-correction can work with sufficient external feedback/tools, which the claim already scopes out by saying "without external feedback"). No evidence found that the claim is outdated, marketing-driven, or unsupported.

confirmed.claim: On GSM8K with GPT-3.5, intrinsic self-correction rounds monotonically decreased accuracy rather than improving it (75.9% standard prompting vs 75.1% after round 1 vs 74.7% after round 2 of self-correction).
confirmed.evidence: Verified directly against the primary source PDF (arxiv.org/pdf/2310.01798, Huang et al., "Large Language Models Cannot Self-Correct Reasoning Yet," ICLR 2024). Table 3 ("Results of GPT-3.5 and GPT-4 on reasoning benchmarks with intrinsic self-correction") lists for GPT-3.5 on GSM8K: Standard Prompting 75.9, Self-Correct (round 1) 75.1, Self-Correct (round 2) 74.7 — an exact, monotonic decrease matching the claim precisely. This is the intrinsic (no oracle-label) self-correction setting, correctly distinguished in the paper from Table 2's oracle-label setting (which shows improvement, 75.9→84.3). The paper's own text corroborates: "after self-correction, the accuracies of all models drop across all benchmarks" and Figure 1 shows GSM8K(GPT-3.5) retains initial answer 74.7% of the time while correct→incorrect changes (8.8%) outnumber incorrect→correct changes (7.0%), causing net degradation. No contradicting evidence found; the claim is a precise, correctly-attributed restatement of a peer-reviewed (ICLR 2024) primary source, not an overreach, misread, or outdated finding.

confirmed.claim: The authors explicitly caution against unguarded optimism about LLMs autonomously self-improving through self-correction loops, directly bearing on how much a self-improvement pipeline can rely on model-internal review versus needing external/deterministic gates.
confirmed.quote: Our research shows that LLMs are not yet capable of self-correcting their reasoning...we urge a more circumspect view on any unwarranted optimism regarding the autonomous evolution of LLMs through self-improvement.
confirmed.evidence: Verified the supporting quote directly against the primary source. Fetching arxiv.org/html/2310.01798v1 (Huang, Chen, Mishra, Zheng, Yu, Song, Zhou — "Large Language Models Cannot Self-Correct Reasoning Yet," ICLR 2024) confirms the Conclusion section reads: "Finally, in light of our findings that LLMs struggle to self-correct reasoning based purely on their inherent capabilities, we urge a more circumspect view on any unwarranted optimism (or fear) regarding the autonomous evolution of LLMs (and AI systems) through self-improvement." The claim's supporting quote is an accurate (if slightly trimmed — drops the parenthetical "(or fear)") rendering of this sentence, not a fabrication or misread. The paper's core empirical finding — intrinsic self-correction (no external feedback) fails and can degrade performance on reasoning benchmarks (GSM8K, CommonSenseQA, HotpotQA) — directly supports the claim's inferential leap that self-improvement pipelines cannot lean on model-internal review alone and need external verification/gates; this is not an overreach but the paper's own stated implication. Checked for staleness: a 2026 search turned up continued 2025-2026 literature (Self-Correction Bench/Tsui 2025 "self-correction blind spot," Zhang et al. 2025 on systematic correct-to-wrong failures, a critical survey "When Can LLMs Actually Correct Their Own Mistakes?") that still treats Huang et al. as the foundational reference and largely confirms the limitation of intrinsic self-correction, with only narrow counter-evidence (e.g., fine-tuning-assisted or external-tool-assisted self-correction methods, which are consistent with — not contradictory to — the claim's external-gate conclusion). Source is a primary, peer-reviewed (ICLR 2024) paper, appropriate strength for the claim being made.

confirmed.claim: The authors justify empirical (test-and-validate) guardrails over formal correctness proofs because proof-based safety for self-modifying code is intractable — directly relevant to the enforcement decision of deterministic gates vs. softer validation.
confirmed.quote: proving that most changes are net beneficial is impossible in practice
confirmed.evidence: Verified against the primary source (arxiv.org/abs/2505.22954, "Darwin Gödel Machine: Open-Ended Evolution of Self-Improving Agents," Zhang, Hu, Lu, Lange, Clune; accepted ICLR 2026). The abstract itself states the traditional Gödel Machine relies on formal proofs that self-modifications are beneficial, but "in practice and without restrictive assumptions about the system, it is impossible to formally prove whether a modification to an AI system will be beneficial" — the quoted phrase "proving that most changes are net beneficial is impossible in practice" is drawn directly from this abstract text (confirmed via WebFetch of the abstract and independently via WebSearch snippet). The paper's proposed alternative, explicitly stated, is that DGM "empirically validates each change using coding benchmarks" rather than relying on formal proof — i.e., exactly the empirical/test-and-validate vs. formal-proof tradeoff the claim describes. This is a primary source, the quote is accurate and in-context (not cherry-picked to misrepresent), and the paper is current (2025 preprint, ICLR 2026 accepted). The claim's substantive assertion (authors justify empirical guardrails over proofs because proof-based safety is intractable) is directly and strongly supported. The added framing ("directly relevant to the enforcement decision of deterministic gates vs. softer validation") is the researcher's own analytical connection to the design question rather than a factual claim from the paper, but it is a reasonable, non-overreaching application — DGM's proof-vs-test tradeoff is analogous, not identical, to deterministic gates vs. prompt discipline, but this is stated as relevance/analogy, not misattributed to the authors.

confirmed.claim: The system self-improves by iteratively modifying its own code and empirically validating each change against coding benchmarks (an evaluator-in-the-loop / proposal-then-validate pattern) rather than applying changes unchecked.
confirmed.quote: iteratively modifies its own code" and "empirically validates each change using coding benchmarks
confirmed.evidence: Claim is supported nearly verbatim by the primary source. Fetching arxiv.org/abs/2505.22954 directly returns the abstract text: "The Darwin Gödel Machine (DGM), a self-improving system that iteratively modifies its own code (thereby also improving its ability to modify its own codebase) and empirically validates each change using coding benchmarks." This is an almost exact match to the claim's supporting quote. Cross-checking the full paper (arxiv.org/html/2505.22954v2) confirms the mechanism is a genuine proposal-then-validate gate, not blind application: "Each newly generated agent is quantitatively evaluated on a chosen coding benchmark to estimate its coding abilities. Only agents that compile successfully and retain the ability to edit a given codebase are added to the DGM archive... All others are discarded." A separate corroborating source (web search summary citing the paper/Sakana AI writeup) adds that all self-modifications and evaluations occur in sandboxed environments under human supervision, further reinforcing the "not applied unchecked" characterization. No contradicting source was found disputing that DGM uses empirical benchmark validation as a gate on self-modification. Paper is a legitimate arXiv preprint (accepted ICLR 2026 per search results), current, and the claim's strength (a factual description of the paper's mechanism, not an extraordinary or safety-clearance claim) matches the primary-source quality of the evidence.

refuted.claim: Multi-turn, underspecified ("Sharded") conversations produce approximately 30% performance degradation compared to single-turn, fully-specified ("Full") interactions, building on and extending Laban et al. (2025)'s finding that LLMs suffer substantial performance drops in multi-turn settings.

The Goal Accessibility Ratio (GAR) — attention from generated tokens back to task-defining instruction tokens — declines monotonically with turn count across every model architecture tested, dropping to 27-48% of its turn-1 value, and this decline is statistically significant.

Under sustained multi-turn interaction, persona/instruction-compliance violation rates converge to a 47-58% range across all four architectures tested once the attention channel to the system-level instructions has closed.

LLMs exhibit a sharp instruction-following performance cliff between turns 10-15 in multi-turn dialogues, evidenced by a new dynamic benchmark (EvolIF) that generates open-ended conversation sequences adapting to model performance.

Even the best-performing model (GPT-5) sustains only ~18.5 turns on average with 70.31% robustness before instruction-following degrades; a second-tier model (Gemini-2.5-Pro) manages only 14.78 turns at 59.90% robustness, with mid-tier models collapsing around 8-10 turns.

There is a consistent trade-off where models that reason more effectively (i.e., score higher on complex reasoning) struggle more to comply with user instructions — reasoning capacity scaling and instruction controllability move in opposite directions.

Not all agentic capabilities degrade equally in multi-turn settings: tool-selection accuracy for top models (GPT-4o, Gemini-2.5-Flash) stayed high (>=97%) even as instruction-following accuracy collapsed for the same models, indicating the degradation is task/constraint-specific rather than a uniform effect of turn count.

Claude Code hooks are shell commands (or HTTP/MCP/prompt/agent handlers) that execute deterministically at defined lifecycle points, explicitly positioned as an alternative to relying on the LLM's own judgment to perform an action.

The Darwin Gödel Machine's self-modification loop was run only under explicit protective controls, not open-ended autonomous self-modification: sandboxing and human oversight were used during all experiments.
